## [00:00 - 03:01]

So in this lesson we're going to take a look at pop-ups and how to best get the most out of the traffic that you're sending to your site. Now pop-ups are really important because it sort of reduces the need to convert everybody the first time they come to your site. It's preferable to do it, but you're not always going to be able to do that. I think it's even more important these days when people are on a train or on a tram on their phone, they see an ad, they click on it, they end up on your website and they're pretty interested, but they may not be somewhere where they want to pull out the credit card and check out where if they can sign up to the pop-up and you email them back within the next 24 hours or 3 hours or something like that and then get them back to your site when they're on a desktop or they're at home on the couch, they may be more comfortable checking out. So we're going to take a look at these pop-ups and just see exactly how powerful they can be. So let's dive in now and have a look at this pop-up. So this is a quad lock pop-up that gets you 10% off and we've been doing this for years now and we've had great success with it. We've been able to grow our email database to quite a substantial level because of the use of these pop-ups. And these pop-ups are getting more and more important with some of the rules that are coming in such as the GDPR and the EU, whereas people when they buy, they have to have an extra thing that makes them subscribe to your list other than just buying and you can't have that little box pre-ticked when they check out anymore. They have to tick that themselves, which is fine and you want to make sure you respect all these laws and look after your customers. But if you can get their permission to send them emails, that's just huge for your business and a pop-up is one of the best ways of doing that by giving them a little bit of value in return. So this pop-up, when you land on our site, we've tested all the different times to pop that up, what pages to pop it up on to get the best results. And it's different for everyone. The best way to do it is to use something like Sumo and A-B test your pop-ups to see what triggers your users. And I know it's different because we get different results based on different countries and different domains that we have this on. Now this pop-up here, for example, I remember the day we put on, no thanks, I'd rather pay full price. That little no thanks, I'd rather pay full price, we took away the little X from the top corner of this pop-up that let you be able to close that pop-up and that was a huge gain changer for us. That had a lift of over 1% in conversion rate of emails and for us over a year, 1% of all the traffic we get to this site turns into tens and tens of thousands of emails. And those tens and tens of thousands of emails with the emails that we send out, they have to turn into orders. So little changes can make huge differences to your business down the track and it's worthwhile putting the effort into work these little hacks out. So if you have a pop-up and you have an X button to close it, people will quite happily

## [03:01 - 06:02]

click on an X to close that pop-up. When they go to have to click on no thanks, I'd rather pay full price. They might just go, you know what, actually, no one wants a pay full price. I'll just chuck my email in. And even if it's only an extra one or two people out of 100 that do that, over a year that amasses a huge amount of people that you can extract a huge amount of value from. So definitely don't write pop-ups off. Definitely take a look at them. So some of the different characteristics that you can tweak and test in your pop-up, everything's from the call to action, the box that they actually put their emails into, the button that they click to subscribe. Now we've test sign up, subscribe and enter. Enter seem to work the best for us, but I think it's different on every website. The way of clearing that pop-up is really important. You can also, big one to test, I think people will forget about, is the grayed out background or the whited out background. What do you do to the rest of the page now? You don't, we've found out that you don't want it to go solid black and just have the pop-up there. We do this light shade of black that you can see through where you know you're on the right page, you know you're just one click away from getting back to where you were and it seems a little bit less invasive, but there's a whole heap of things you can have really strong branding. You could have some snazzy call to action. We've tried some jokey lines and things like that, but from what we've got right now is kind of simple. In places like Europe you might need to include your GDPR warnings and things like that. And then you've got to work out what's the best way of including those as well to stay within the law and make sure you're giving, you'll use what they deserve, but at the same time making sure you're maximizing the results every time you do a pop-up. Something else we play with with our pop-ups is running different tests and different versions for both desktop and mobile and we also can run different versions of the pop-up that may work better in some countries and not as good in others. Something else you can do with Sumo is you can IP target people. So even if someone from Europe ends up on our .com site, they will still get the European pop-up that is full GDPR compliant. And so we're still saying within all the rules and regs, which are important, especially as your business scales. If you're running pop-ups in Europe and if you're collecting emails in Europe, you must make sure that your GDPR compliant and when you're a smaller business, you probably get away with this, that and the other thing. But as your business scales, you become more important, become more of a target, you've got to make sure that you're staying compliant along the way. So the cool thing about AB testing of a pop-up is that it's sort of the cream on top of everything else you're doing. So you're still spending your money on Facebook or Google ads and you're still writing blogs and pumping traffic through your websites as much as you can.
